results.title: Batch 11 Multi-Target Dataset and Metric Validation Program
results.humanReadableSummary: Batch 11 runs a focused dataset-and-metric validation program across four public classification targets in one domain: tabular and image-derived benchmark validation.
results.summary: Batch 11 runs a focused dataset-and-metric validation program across four public classification targets in one domain: tabular and image-derived benchmark validation.
limitations: Limitations
The schema tool is not a semantic leakage detector.
The metric tool is not a proof of absence of leakage.

The toolchain is useful, but mostly as evidence packaging and anti-hype support. On these single-table targets, pandas and sklearn explained most raw findings. The custom tools added source-hash-bound evidence cards, explicit dummy and shuffled-label controls, seed/split stress, replay notes, and public limitation discipline.
The schema tool did not beat pandas for raw discovery. Its value was hash-bound packaging, repeatable comparison, and public limitation discipline.

results.title: Batch 13 Protocol-First Benchmark Validation Week 1
results.humanReadableSummary: Batch 13 is the first execution batch of the Protocol-First Benchmark Validation and Split-Risk Program selected in Batch 12.
results.summary: Batch 13 is the first execution batch of the Protocol-First Benchmark Validation and Split-Risk Program selected in Batch 12.
limitations: The linear and tree baselines both stayed far above dummy baselines. The result is not a benchmark win; it is a protocol-vs-random split comparison.
Limitations
The random challenger uses one stratified random split family and seed/stress checks; it is not an exhaustive split search.
`metric_stress_validator` was used as anti-hype support, not as proof of benchmark validity.

Shuffled-label controls remained low on all completed targets. The useful stress finding was not a magic discovery; it was that random split performance differed materially from source train/test performance on all three targets.
The source train/test split can be followed, but this is not a full paper benchmark reproduction.

results.title: Batch 14 Protocol Risk Expansion Week 2
results.humanReadableSummary: Batch 14 expands the Protocol-First Benchmark Validation program from the three Batch 13 targets to four targets with harder split-risk coverage.
results.summary: Batch 14 expands the Protocol-First Benchmark Validation program from the three Batch 13 targets to four targets with harder split-risk coverage.
limitations: Limitations
This is not a full official benchmark reproduction or paper reproduction.
RandomForest on Shuttle was fit on a stratified 20,000-row training subset to keep execution bounded; Logistic/linear source-vs-random comparisons were the primary split-risk signal.

It is not a roadmap, continuity note, benchmark-win claim, or full official benchmark reproduction. The executed claim is narrower: Sovryn followed or approximated public source-described split protocols, compared them with same-size stratified-random challengers, and scored split-risk severity.

results.title: Batch 24 Protocol-Card Kill Week and Next Frontier
results.humanReadableSummary: Batch 24 attacked Batches 21-23 plus prior protocol-risk evidence from Batch 20, Batch 15 Shuttle, and Batch 16 Landsat. It preserved Protocol Cards as useful infrastructure with caveats, downgraded ambiguous-card claims, and selected Benchmark Leakage-Risk Detection under Protocol Cards as the next program.
results.summary: Batch 24 attacked Batches 21-23 plus prior protocol-risk evidence from Batch 20, Batch 15 Shuttle, and Batch 16 Landsat. limitations: Batch 23 did not resolve Vehicle ambiguity; it documented and quantified it. That is a useful negative result. Future Protocol-Card work should treat ambiguous targets as separate ambiguity studies or filter them out of scaled replay until external protocol evidence is added.

Accuracy, macro-F1, weighted-F1, and per-class summaries do not always tell the same story. Macro-F1 remains valuable for class-risk detection, but Batch 24 narrows metric claims: metric_stress_validator is anti-hype support, not an automatic leakage detector or official benchmark validator.

Vehicle ambiguity is valuable because it prevents false clarity. Low-risk controls are valuable because they limit overgeneralization. Replay partials are valuable because they separate deterministic code replay from external-source stability. Metric-risk downgrades are valuable because they keep macro-F1 and accuracy claims bounded.
Publication criteria: every result must include source cards, protocol cards, leakage-risk evidence, baselines, negative controls, limitations, and no benchmark-win claim.

Limitations

Sovryn attempted bounded original conceptual discovery by mining 100 anomalies, generating 50 principle candidates, freezing 24 non-obvious predictions, executing 18 fresh external prediction tests, designing and running six decisive experiments, and publishing a paper-style result. limitations: The Receipt-Stable Descriptor Residual Candidate says that a descriptor residual is only a credible bounded discovery signal when the public-source receipt is stable under retrieval or replay, the descriptor is explicitly defined, and the residual persists against simple baselines or controls. If the source receipt changes, the descriptor is underspecified, or a trivial baseline explains the residual, the candidate must be narrowed, downgraded, or treated as not supported.
Decision boundary: residual language is not allowed to strengthen the candidate when receipt stability, descriptor definition, or baseline comparison is missing.
